Scenario.title: Healthcare Sector Phishing Attack Leading to Malware Deployment (Campaign C0026)
Scenario.overview: Healthcare and Public Health Intermediate A sophisticated phishing campaign targeting healthcare organizations uses social engineering to deliver malware, escalating privileges and evading defenses before manipulating critical patient data.

threat_actor_profile.motivation: Financial gain through data extortion and ransomware

stage.name: Initial Access
stage.description: Attackers send spoofed emails impersonating healthcare administrators, embedding malicious Microsoft Office documents with obfuscated macros. Recipients opening these attachments trigger initial code execution via embedded scripts.

stage.name: Execution
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 44: Overflow Binary Resource File
stage.description: KOPILUWAK malware is activated through macro exploitation, leveraging a buffer overflow vulnerability in document parsing to inject and execute arbitrary code in memory.

stage.name: Persistence
stage.description: The malware creates a scheduled task in Windows Task Scheduler to ensure persistence across system reboots, using stolen credentials to maintain access.

stage.name: Privilege Escalation
stage.description: Legacy medical device firmware vulnerabilities are exploited to escalate privileges, granting administrative access to hospital network management systems.

stage.name: Defense Evasion
stage.capec: CAPEC ID: 636: Hiding Malicious Data or Code within Files
stage.description: Encrypted payloads are hidden within legitimate-looking log files, while communication with command-and-control servers uses domain fronting to avoid detection by network monitoring tools.

stage.name: Impact
stage.description: Patient records are altered and exfiltrated using QUIETCANARY RAT, disrupting clinical workflows and encrypting data for ransom demands.

Scenario.summary: Campaign C0026 exploited healthcare workers through phishing to deploy malware, escalated privileges via legacy systems, and manipulated critical data, demonstrating a multi-stage attack tailored to the sector's operational dependencies.
